segments.text: [MUSIC PLAYING]

JAMAL EASON: Hi,
welcome to What's

New in Android
Development Tools.

I'm Jamal, Director of Product
Management on the Android
Development Experience team.
TOR NORBYTE: And I'm Tor.

I'm the Engineering Director
for Android Developer Tools
and Libraries.
[APPLAUSE, CHEERING]

JAMAL EASON: So
together, we're going

to walk through some updates
to Android Studio, where

our mission is to make it easy
for you to make high quality

apps for Android across a whole
portfolio of Android devices.

First up, I'll cover a quick
update to the product roadmap,

and then Tor will give a
demo of some of the latest
features and action.

And lastly, I'll wrap up and
give some additional feature
updates.

OK, first up, let's take a
quick peek at the roadmap.

Now, as a reminder, we
have doubled our releases

of Android Studio where the
initial platform release

is really focused on getting
Android Studio aligned

to the latest version of
the intelligent platform.

Plus, it includes a handful of
bugs and product quality fixes.

Then, we follow up
with a feature drop

release, which includes
Android-specific features.

So since I/O last year, we
went through a whole release

cycle of Android Studio
Ladybug and Meerkat.

So let me walk
through a few updates
that you might have missed.

For Android Studio Ladybug
feature drop release,

we have a handful of features
around Wear OS Preview & Health
Services.

Plus, for those of you who use
ads in your apps and games,

we improve the App
Links Assistant

to make it even easier
to test your app links.

And we integrated Google Play
SDK Insights right into the IDE,

so you don't have to go
to the Play Dev Console

to see potential
publishing hurdles.

Lastly, we spent a
notable amount of time

on addressing over
700 bugs, both

reported by you and our
internal quality team.

For the Android Studio
Meerkat release,

we are continuing to invest in
more refined tools, such as,

Jetpack Compose Preview
Enhancements, and a new template

for adding Kotlin Multiplatform
to existing projects.

Plus, we also updated
the Build Menu

to be more efficient and more
streamlined in this release.

And across all these
releases, we've

been building on
our capabilities

of AI-powered Developer
Assistance with Gemini
and Android Studio.

From code suggestions,
generating documentation,

to solving crashes, we've
applied Gemini and Android

Studio to a wide range of
your core developer workflows.

Now, with AI, we know
this is an evolving space

with almost every couple
of weeks a new improvement
to the Gemini model.

Our philosophy with AI is that
it's not a one size fits all.
Let me explain.

So in some cases,
we know you want

to go from a prototype,
a new idea on our API.

It needs to go from zero to 1
and rapidly think of a new idea.

While on the other hand, you
have an existing code base

that you need help with
taking your idea from one

to production, a production
environment where you have

your core app business,
where you probably

have a higher bar for
quality and stability.

So for the Android
Studio team we

have what we're delivering on
a wide range of features that
address all these mindsets.

Today, we'll show you some of
the latest AI-enabled features

we've been working on in
Android Studio Narwhal,
plus some other surprises.

And in addition to AI work, we
show you some new improvements
in other critical workflows.

OK, now, I have Tor to walk us
through some of the new features
in Android Studio.

And after we finish
the demo, I'll

walk through some
additional product updates.
OK, Tor, take it away.
TOR NORBYTE: All right.
Good morning, everybody.
So I have a lot to show.
[APPLAUSE, CHEERING]
Thanks.

And I'm actually going to start
with AI features this time.

But before I get into some
of the bigger features,

I just want to highlight some
of the usability improvements

we made, because I just think
it makes it nicer to use.

So, yes, you can see
the screen already.

So the first one I'll talk
about is code completion.

So we're now doing syntax
highlighting on the ghost text.

So you can see it's subtle,
but it's sort of translucent.

And I think it just makes it
a lot easier to visually scan.

And in chat, we've rewritten
the entire thing in Compose.

So we now have nice
animations and motion.

When I run a query here, you
can see I can interrupt it,
another nice animation.

We are now streaming
code snippets

so they appear more immediately,
and the scrolling behavior
is better.

So you don't have to
scroll back up to see
the beginning of the reply.

We also have a
context drawer, so you

can see which files
are implicitly
attached to your queries.

And you can also go and
attach your own files

if you want to ask
about an image,

for example, to
convert it to code.

And notice how we're now able
to actually use Gemini 2.5 Pro.

So we're going to start
showing the model you're using
in the bottom right corner.

So let me just say,
silly question, hello.

Look at the model selector
when I press Hello.
See the nice shimmer?
I like small stuff like that.
All right.

Anyway, Gemini 2.5
Pro is enabling

us to write some much more
advanced features than before.

And so the first feature I
want to show you like that
is our update assistant.

So we know that library
updates are a pain.

We've heard that loud
and clear, especially,

when there are source-breaking
changes, which there often are.

So the Upgrade
Assistant is basically
trying to automate that for you.
So here we have a TOML file.

We can see lots of
dependency warnings.

And a new improvement in
the latest version of lint

is that it doesn't just
check the maven.google.com

dependencies, it checks, well,
for example, Maven Central, too.

So we can see we have a
new version of Kotlin.

So now, I can apply a quick
fix, update all libraries
with Gemini.

When I do that, we are
analyzing the build files.

You can see it's now proposing
